summary: DNS hijacked during ICO, routing users to an attacker-controlled site where they sent ETH to the wrong address.
DNS hijacked to serve a fake site that prompted users to enter private keys, compromising credentials.
DNS hijacked to serve a cloned frontend that tricked users into entering private keys and approving malicious transfers.
DNS hijacked, replacing/redirecting the wallet site to steal user funds.
After repeated compromises, users were forced onto alternative community frontends (ForkDelta) or had to withdraw via direct contract calls through block explorers.
BGP/DNS routing manipulation sent users to a fraudulent MEW clone, resulting in fund theft during a limited attack window.

SEC settled with EtherDelta's founder for operating an unregistered exchange — an early enforcement action focused on the web-based frontend + smart contract model, foreshadowing later geofencing patterns.
DNS compromised on both protocols, routing users to malicious look-alike frontends with wallet-drain risk.
Exchange decommissioned; users told to withdraw funds from account and contract balances. UI kept online temporarily, then removed.
Uniswap Labs removed certain token categories (synthetic/derivative-like assets) from the hosted interface. Contracts persisted, but the official UI delisted them.
Blocked US users on the hosted frontend. Contracts and aggregator routes still existed, but the main UI denied access to US-based users.

Attacker injected malicious JavaScript into Badger's web interface via Cloudflare-related access, prompting users to sign approvals that enabled massive theft. Many victims were Gnosis Safe multisig users.
Infura configuration changes cut access from certain sanctioned jurisdictions. Because many DeFi UIs depend on hosted RPC, this functionally took dApps offline for impacted users.
Sanctioned by OFAC as part of the Tornado-style pattern: sanctions lead to infrastructure/platform removals and official access paths disappearing.
DNS hijacked; SlowMist reported over $2M stolen and warned users not to interact with the site.
Web infrastructure compromised to serve malicious UI content, resulting in theft of user funds.
Third-party script/ad injection displayed malicious 'Connect wallet' popups on CoinGecko and Etherscan, putting users at risk of wallet draining.
Attackers accessed DNS/registrar controls via Namecheap and redirected multiple DeFi sites to inject malicious approval prompts. Multiple projects affected simultaneously.
Ankr-hosted RPC gateway domains for Polygon and Fantom were DNS-hijacked, pointing users and dApps at attacker infrastructure that prompted malicious interactions.
Attacker compromised PREMINT's official site and injected malicious prompts, stealing user funds during the incident window.

Following OFAC sanctions, Tornado Cash's GitHub repos were removed/suspended and hosted UI became unreliable. Users forced to use alternative UIs, IPFS/ENS mirrors, or direct contract interaction to withdraw.
Uniswap's hosted interface introduced address screening, blocking certain wallets associated with sanctions from using the UI.
Aave's interface-level blocking of certain addresses (post-sanctions/dusting era) showed how UI gating works even when core contracts remain permissionless.
DNS attacked at registrar level; users served a malicious Curve clone that prompted approvals and drained funds.
BGP/DNS-style attack gave attacker control of the official site, inducing users to approve malicious spender contracts.
UI and Discord shut down by Nov 15, 2022. Users told to withdraw remaining funds from garden contracts via Etherscan 'write contract' calls.
Frontend/Cloudflare compromise presented malicious UI to a small number of users. Two users affected; one reimbursed. Contracts were not exploited.
Solana-based vaults ("Volts") moved into withdrawal-only mode. Deposits disabled; UI became an exit ramp only.
Phased UI restrictions: functionality narrowed from full to unstake/unwrap to claim-only. Continued access depended on community-hosted UI.
UI sunset announced and pools immediately set to withdraw-only mode.
Bridge operational collapse due to team/ops failure (MPC/relayers/validation). Even with contracts still existing, users were stuck with delayed or impossible withdrawals, leading to major losses.
CFTC obtained a default judgment including website/interface shutdown language — a rare case of a US regulator pursuing a DAO and ordering a DeFi UI offline.
CFTC cease-and-desist orders prompted teams to tighten US geofencing or remove products from hosted UIs to reduce regulatory exposure.
Registrar compromised via social engineering; DNS pointed to a phishing clone with a wallet drainer flow. Balancer published a postmortem and advised token revocations.
Domain/DNS rerouted to a phishing site during a defined window, draining user funds.
Lido DAO approved winding down the Solana deployment, forcing users into exit procedures rather than normal ongoing UI usage.
DELV placed a banner warning the Element UI would be taken down and published a step-by-step Etherscan withdrawal guide for users to retrieve their funds.
DNS attacked on both domains simultaneously; attacker-run clones served to users. Estimated up to ~$250k in damage.
Malicious version of Ledger's widely-used wallet-connection library distributed via CDN, instantly propagating drainer prompts across many dApps. Systemic supply-chain risk realized.
Highly targeted Safe{Wallet} frontend/code tampering in the transaction-signing flow enabled the largest crypto heist on record. Exchange/custody incident using Safe multisig UI manipulation.
Registrar-level DNS attack again; malicious clone attempted wallet draining. Curve was forced to move to a new domain (curve.finance).
"Closing the Jar": UI disabled after Oct 1, 2025. Users urged to withdraw from Jars; post-shutdown claims only possible via contract interaction and posted Merkle proofs.
Domain/registrar takeover campaign targeting Squarespace-managed domains (following Google Domains migration). DNS redirected to wallet-draining phishing kits across multiple projects.
dYdX v3 website/domain compromised to deliver attacker content. dYdX published a postmortem.
Registrar account compromised; frontend taken offline. Users warned not to interact as frontend exploit conditions existed.
v3 product sunset with a fixed halt date (Oct 28). Trading, oracle updates, and funding halted; users urged to withdraw USDC by the deadline.
Published dedicated withdrawal interfaces and guidance following farewell announcement. Emphasized structured offboarding as team stepped away from DELV-built protocols.
DNS hijacked again (second time); phishing clone served to users. Over $1M stolen in a short window before teams and partners mitigated.
Staking via UI ended; withdrawals supported via Polygon Lido UI until Jun 16, 2025. After that date, users must use block explorer tools.
Hard deadline: repay/withdraw by Dec 19 via UI. Afterwards, protocol access only possible directly via smart contracts — much higher friction for average users.
Bridge/network wind-down after Alameda collapse. Users had to unwind positions before deadlines because the system depended on off-chain coordination that was no longer viable.
DNS for openeden.com and portal.openeden.com compromised; Blockaid flagged active frontend drainer activity. Users urged not to interact.
Frontend drainer activity detected; users warned not to sign or interact. Reported as thwarted with no funds lost, but a confirmed frontend compromise attempt.
Markets set to 0% LTV during wind-down; users publicly urged to withdraw funds.
